hook: You already know that statistics can reveal powerful truths — but the quality of any conclusion depends entirely on how the data was gathered in the first place. A brilliant analysis built on flawed data is like a skyscraper on a cracked foundation. In this unit, you'll discover how researchers design studies that produce trustworthy results, and how to spot the hidden traps that can silently corrupt data.
concept: Every topic here revolves around one central question: how do we collect data in a way that lets us draw valid, unbiased conclusions? This is the discipline of study design, formally captured by the principle that structure and randomness must work together to eliminate systematic error.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to distinguish between sampling techniques — such as simple random, stratified, and cluster sampling — and identify how each can introduce or minimize bias in a dataset.
Master the critical difference between observational studies and controlled experiments, and understand why only one of them can establish cause-and-effect relationships.
Apply the three pillars of sound experimental design — control, randomization, and replication — to evaluate and construct experiments that produce reliable, reproducible results.
You will learn to identify confounding variables that lurk behind apparent relationships and explain how proper study design guards against drawing false causal conclusions.
Analyze how question wording, ordering, and response options in surveys can introduce bias, and apply best practices to design questionnaires that capture honest, accurate responses.
